coredata.dc:title: Chitosan microspheres loaded with 5Fu and Polydopamine-carbon dots for enhancing chemo-photothermal therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fang
Chen, Naipin
Li, Jiarui
Xu, Ziqi
coredata.dc:description: Herein, Carbon dots (CDS) was modified with polydopamine (PDA), and they were encapsulated using chitosan (CS) to prepare dual photosensitizers drug loaded microspheres (CS@CDs-PDA + 5Fu NPs). The microsphere has an average particle size of 255 nm and photothermal conversion efficiency of 69.6 % under 808 nm NIR. Notably, the microsphere showed pH and photothermal response drug release performance, contributing to synergistic chemo-photothermal treatment for tumor cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Polydopamine
Photothermal therapy
Drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of drug loaded chitosan microspheres
In vitro drug release of microspheres

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (21KJA220004). We thank very much advanced analysis and testing center of Nanjing Forestry University for TEM measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Construction procedures of drug loaded chitosan microspheres; (b) IR spectra of CDs, DA, CDs-PDA, CS@CDs, CS@CDs-PDA; (c) (d)(e) TEM images of CDs, CS@CDs and CS@CDs-PDA (inset: the particle size distribution of the samples).

(a)The temperature curves of different formulations (H2O, CDs, CDs-PDA, CS@CDs and CS@CDs-PDA) with increased NIR irradiation time. (b) (c) Photothermal curves of CS@CDs-PDA with different concentrations and different power densities. (d) Photos monitored by an IR thermal camera at various times. (e) (f) Temperature variation curves of photothermal cycle experiment of CS@CDs and CS@CDs-PDA.

(a) In vitro drug release curve of CS@CDs-PDA + 5Fu NPs samples in different pH buffer solutions; (b) Release curves of CS@CDs + 5Fu NPs and CS@CDs-PDA + 5Fu NPs in pH 5.67. (Inset: the first 4 h drug release curves).
Survival rate of HepG2 cells (a) at different concentrations (power density: 1.5 W/cm2) and (b) at different power densities (5Fu: 50 mg/ml).

head.ce:title: Chitosan microspheres loaded with 5Fu and Polydopamine-carbon dots for enhancing chemo-photothermal therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Provincial Key Lab for the Chemistry and Utilization of Agro-forest Biomass, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Provincial Key Lab for the Chemistry and Utilization of Agro-forest Biomass

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu provincial key lab for the chemistry and utilization of agro-forest biomass, Jiangsu, RP China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, Carbon dots (CDS) was modified with polydopamine (PDA), and they were encapsulated using chitosan (CS) to prepare dual photosensitizers drug loaded microspheres (CS@CDs-PDA + 5Fu NPs). The microsphere has an average particle size of 255 nm and photothermal conversion efficiency of 69.6 % under 808 nm NIR. Notably, the microsphere showed pH and photothermal response drug release performance, contributing to synergistic chemo-photothermal treatment for tumor cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer has been one of the most dangerous diseases that threaten human safety [1]. Because of its unique targeting and simplicity, chemotherapy plays a pivotal role in cancer treatment [2]. Recent years, a large number of new technologies have been developed, among which photothermal therapy (PPT) has been widely concerned by researchers [3]. Therefore, researchers proposed to combine PTT with chemotherapy and put forward a synergistic treatment method, which can not only improve the efficiency of treatment but also reduce the side effects of drugs on human body.

There is a large number of sp3 and sp2 as well as π - electrons in carbon quantum dots (CDs), which allow CDs to trigger the absorbed light energy to convert into heat or release the acceptor photons, thus exhibiting good photothermal transition, at the same time, CDs has the advantages of low toxicity and great biocompatibility, so they have many applications for PTT therapy [4] and Biosensing [5]. Polydopamine (PDA) inspired by mussels is another good photosensitizer, which not only has excellent biocompatibility [6] but also has good photothermal conversion ability [7]. Meanwhile, PDA has excellent adhesion performance and can be attached to the surface of many substances. In photothermal therapy, light irradiated dual photosensitizers to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) [8] in cells, which can effectively inhibit the growth of cancer cells and promote cancer apoptosis.

We have investigated drug loaded chitosan nanoparticles using emulsion crosslinking method in previous study. In this study, CDs was modified with PDA, 5Fu was the anticancer drug model, and then they were encapsulated by chitosan to prepare chitosan complex microspheres (CS@CDs-PDA + 5Fu NPs). The effect of the dual photosensitizers on photothermal performance and in vitro drug release were investigated. Additionally, the combined chemo-photothermal effect on Hep G2 cells was also analyzed, further manifesting that it was potential drug delivery microsphere for cancer synthetic treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of drug loaded chitosan microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) was carried out according to the study [9], 3.44 g citric acid and 3.005 g urea was dissolved in 20 ml deionized water and reacted for 8 h under 200 ℃. CDs-PDA was synthesized by adding 30 mg CDs and 150 mg dopamine hydrochloride in ammonia for 7 h at 40℃. The drug loaded microspheres CS@CDs and CS@CDs-PDA were prepared via emulsion crosslinking method of our previous study [10]. Briefly, 60 mg CDs or CDs-PDA was added in 6 ml chitosan solution (2 %) for ultrasonic emulsification for 3 min. Simultaneously, 24 ml liquid paraffin with 100 uL glutaraldehyde and span-80 was ultrasonic emulsification for 3 min. Then, solution containing chitosan and CDs/CDs-PDA was added drop by drop to liquid paraffin emulsion, and reacted for 2 h. Finally, the suspension was centrifuged and washed with petroleum ether.

The nanoparticles morphology was characterized using TEM. FTIR analysis was performed by FT-IR360 instrument. The photothermal measurement was irradiated using 808 nm NIR laser for 5 min at different power densities, and FLIR E40 camera was used to measure temperature and photos. In vitro drug release experiments were accomplished in pH 7.4, 6.58, 5.67 with or without NIR, and drug quantity was analyzed using UV–visible spectrophotometer. HepG2 cells were used to investigate the cytotoxicity of nanoparticles through MTT assay.

Fig. 1 (a) displayed the fabrication mechanism of our drug-loaded microspheres. As shown in Fig. 1(b), the main absorption peaks of CDs are carboxyl group at 3050 cm−1 and carbon skeleton at 1610 cm−1, while at DA sample spectra, the absorption peaks of amino group at 3346, 1610, 878 and 815 cm−1, phenolic hydroxyl group at 3225 and 1286 cm−1 are characteristic peaks. In CDs-PDA IR spectra, the absorption peaks of amino group disappeared at 3346 cm−1 and amide bonds appeared at 1694 cm−1, indicating that dopamine was successfully polymerized with CDs. After they were encapsulated with chitosan, the characteristic peak of saturated –CH2- in chitosan at 2925 cm−1 and COC- at 1070 cm−1 were generated in both microsphere spectra. Fig. 1(c) displayed carbon dots TEM with average particle size of 37.8 nm, while the particle size increased to 220 and 255 nm of CS@CDs and CS@CDs-PDA microspheres from Fig. 1(d) and Fig. 1(e).

Considering that CDs-based microspheres could be used as effective PTT agents, the photothermal properties of four samples were explored with an 808 nm laser irradiation at same concentration and power density, water as the control group. Fig. 2 (a) was the temperature curves and Fig. 2(d) displayed samples photos monitored by an IR thermal camera at various times. The CS@CDs-PDA sample could reach 59 °C contrast with other photothermal agent, which was the highest temperature after NIR light exposure for 5 min, indicating it had great potential to deliver high thermal energy to kill cancer cells. This is benefited from the combination of two excellent photothermal agents, CDs and PDA. Subsequently, the photothermal performance of CS@CDs-PDA NPs at different power densities or concentrations were investigated. The results shown in Fig. 2(b)-(c) proved the temperature would increase as power densities or concentrations increased. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 2e and f, both CS@CDs-PDA and CS@CDs nanoparticles displayed well photothermal stability during five laser on/off cycles, with no apparent temperature attenuation. Furthermore, the photothermal conversion efficiency of CS@CDs-PDA was 69.6 %, higher than CS@CDs of 31.3 %, also confirming dual photosensitizer possessed constant photothermal conversion capacity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug release of microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 (a) shows in vitro drug release performance of CS@CDs-PDA + 5Fu NPs samples under different buffer conditions, with or without NIR. The cumulative drug release rate in pH 7.4 was 40.8 % at 48 h without NIR irradiation, while it could reach 58.9 % with NIR irradiation, indicating photothermal effect of the microsphere could increase drug release. The reason maybe the degradation rate of chitosan accelerates and promotes the movement of small molecule drugs in microspheres, so as to accelerate drug release. Under the condition of tumor weak acid pH 6.58 and pH 5.67, the cumulative drug release rate of the experimental group became faster, which could be beneficial for anticancer effect. It is due to the amino protonation of chitosan in acidic environment destroying the hydrogen bond between inter-molecules, making the microspheres easier to crack. Furthermore, the drug release of two drug loaded microspheres CS@CDs and CS@CDs-PDA were investigated in pH 5.67 buffer solution, and the results are shown in Fig. 3b. The two curves displayed the same release trend and the cumulative drug release was significantly increased with NIR irradiated at 1, 5, 10 and 30 h, but CS@CDs-PDA + 5Fu NPs sample increased more. After 48 h of drug release, the cumulative drug release rates of CS@CDs + 5Fu NPs and CS@CDs-PDA + 5Fu NPs were 64.6 % and 76.9 %, respectively. It was because the NIR irradiation temperature increased more when the dual photosensitizer was used together, which made the small molecule drug in the microsphere move faster and the drug release rate was higher. The results indicated that the synergistic effect of CDs and PDA could accelerate drug release and it was beneficial for tumor cells inhibition.

HepG2 cells were used as cell models in cytotoxicity experiment. MTT method was used to investigate the chemo-photothermal combination therapy on tumor cells of drug loaded microspheres CS@CDs-PDA. According to the data in Fig. 4 , the inhibition rate of cells in each experimental group increased with the increase of 5Fu concentration or NIR irradiation power density. When 5Fu concentration was 100 μg/ml, the inhibition rate of CS@CDs-PDA + 5Fu NPs group was 68.1 %, and it increased to 97.6 % with 1.5 W/cm2 NIR irradiation. Furthermore, in the experimental group without anti-tumor drugs, photothermal therapy alone could also produce certain inhibitory effect on tumor cells, for instance, the inhibition rate was 27.1 % with 1.5 W/cm2 NIR for 5 min, and the inhibition rate could increase to 88.8 % after loaded 50 mg/ml drug in the microspheres. However, CS@CDs-PDA + 5Fu NPs group was 59.9 % inhibition rate with the same drug concentration and without NIR illumination. The results suggest that NIR irradiation intensifies the drug's damage to cells, it can not only inhibit tumor cells, but also achieve synergistic therapeutic effect with chemotherapy drug. The IC50 values of CS@CDs-PDA + 5Fu NPs with or without NIR were 25.82 and 4.41 μg/ml, which were calculated using GraphPad Prism8 software according to cell inhibition rates of different 5Fu concentrations, also indicating chemotherapy and photothermal synergistic therapeutic could better induce cancer cells apoptosis.

Considering photothermal conversion efficiency of drug-loaded microspheres to be increased, dual photosensitizer was prepared by polymerizing dopamine on CDs surface, and then CS@CDs-PDA + 5Fu NPs was prepared using emulsion crosslinking method. In the photothermal experiment, the photothermal conversion efficiency increased from 31.3 % to 69.6 %, and the temperature could easily reach above 50℃ to meet the conditions of cell apoptosis. In vitro drug release results showed that the microspheres had obvious pH response and photothermal response. MTT experiments displayed that the Hep G2 cell inhibition rate could prominently increase with chemotherapy and photothermal therapy synergistic treatment.

Fang Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Naipin Chen: Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jiarui Li: Data curation. Ziqi Xu: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (21KJA220004). We thank very much advanced analysis and testing center of Nanjing Forestry University for TEM measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2022.133167.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
